problem_statement:
`EventSourcedRememberEntitiesShardStore` not honouring `max-updates-per-write` setting

Upon inspection of the [`RememberEntitiesShardStore.Update` handler in `EventSourcedRememberEntitiesShardStore`](https://github.com/akka/akka/blob/aaf7b8748dbd0622db0dbee9c91e1f646497c8e5/akka-cluster-sharding/src/main/scala/akka/cluster/sharding/internal/EventSourcedRememberEntitiesShardStore.scala#L94) it appears that the code is not doing what was originally intended in #29233.
It appears the `events` list will only ever contain 1 `EntitiesStarted` and/or 1 `EntitiesRemoved` each with the ids of all the entities started or removed, irrespective of their count. 
The later code that was intended to split the entities across multiple writes thus takes no notice of the number of entities in each of those events and thus `max-updates-per-write` will not have any effect.
This means with a large number of simultaneous entity activations or deactivations the journal could receive huge events to persist.

Scala 3 source incompatibilities around implicit ActorSystem

* `Tcp()` does not work any more but requires an explicit ActorSystem.
 * Automatic conversion between classic and typed ActorSystem does not work implicitly any more, e.g. `Http()` does not work sometimes when only a classic ActorSystem is implicitly in scope.

Inconsistent OSGi metadata regarding scala-java8-compat

In commit 8468e6ae the dependency to `scala-java8-compat` has been updated to version 1.0.0. `Dependencies` contains code to define the version based on the version of Scala in use; for Scala 3 and Scala 2.13, the version is now set to 1.0.0.

In `OSGi`, however, the version range to this dependency is still set to the interval [0.8.0, 1.0.0) - excluding version 1.0.0. This means that in order to fulfill this requirement, in an OSGi environment a 0.x version of `scala-java8-compat` needs to be deployed, which is inconsistent with the dependency declaration.

To make the OSGi metadata consistent with the dependency declaration, the version range in `OSGi` could be increased to include 1.x versions of the library. Alternatively, the range could also be computed dynamically based on the Scala version.

Note: The constellation worked before upgrading to version 1.0.0, because then the versions 0.8.0 and 0.9.x were used for Scala 2.12 and Scala 2.13 respective. Both of these versions are included in the range [0.8.0, 1.0.0) defined within `OSGi`.

StreamConverters.asOutputStream() produces an empty ByteString when an empty array is wrote to the OutputStream

`StreamConverters.asOutputStream()` produces an empty `ByteString` when an empty array is wrote to the `OutputStream`.

Akka version: 2.6.17

Scastie snippet: https://scastie.scala-lang.org/BQChTrWNQc2xRGwRWWSJGw

It causes problems e.g. when used with Play's `Ok.chunked(...)` because it requires non-empty `ByteString`s, otherwise it throws `java.lang.AssertionError: assertion failed: Http chunks must not be empty`.

The default implementation of `OutputStream` ignores empty arrays and this one should as well.

JoinConfigCompatChecker consumes too much CPU on cluster join

I profiled JVM in a small Akka cluster (9 nodes, Kubernetes, 100m CPU request) that had difficulties forming a cluster and discovered that nodes were spending a lot of (> 80% for several minutes) of CPU time in `JoinConfigCompatChecker` doing `List.contains(...)`. Giving more CPU resources to those k8s pods would solve this in our case. That's not optimal since these are small test clusters. This is a ticket to improve the performance of `JoinConfigCompatChecker`.

large-message-destinations not supported for response to ask

[Large message destinations](https://doc.akka.io/docs/akka/current/remoting-artery.html#dedicated-subchannel-for-large-messages) can be configured to send larger messages (by default >256KB and <2MB) to remote actors. This does not work in combination with ClusterSharding.

I've created a [minimal test case](https://github.com/an-tex/akka-samples/blob/2.6/akka-sample-cluster-scala/src/multi-jvm/scala/sample/cluster/stats/LargeMessageSpec.scala) reproducing the issue using a multi jvm test with a three node cluster. It's based on this [existing test case](https://github.com/akka/akka-samples/blob/2.6/akka-sample-cluster-scala/src/multi-jvm/scala/sample/cluster/stats/StatsSampleSpec.scala).

Inside this repo run `sbt multi-jvm:testOnly *.LargeMessageSpec` and you'll see the error

`[JVM-3] akka.remote.OversizedPayloadException: Discarding oversized payload sent to Some(Actor[akka://LargeMessageSpec@127.0.0.1:61038/system/sharding/largeMessageTypeKey#-806848135]): max allowed size 262144 bytes. Message type [akka.cluster.sharding.typed.ShardingEnvelope].`

even though there's a [large destination configured](https://github.com/an-tex/akka-samples/blob/2.6/akka-sample-cluster-scala/src/main/resources/application.conf#L23) like this:

```
akka.remote.artery.large-message-destinations = [
  "/system/singletonManagerlargeMessageSingleton/*",
  "/system/sharding/largeMessageTypeKey*"
]
```

Note: This error occurs only on two of the nodes as serialization occurs only between JVM boundaries 

To make sure the `large-message-destinations` are actually working at all there's a separate test case using a ClusterSingleton where no issues occur.

Versions:
```scala
val akkaVersion = "2.6.14"
scalaVersion := "2.13.5"
```

`FlatMap.map2Eval` is not lazy and blows the stack in cases where it probably shouldn't

The current implementation of the `FlatMap.map2Eval` is the following:
https://github.com/typelevel/cats/blob/8681c88a40327ae7682bc5cd2feed4eec9e7829d/core/src/main/scala/cats/FlatMap.scala#L116-L117

If that implementation is used inside `Foldable.foldRight` it blows the stack very easily:
```scala
val G = Applicative[OptionT[Id, *]]

Foldable[List]
  .foldRight(List.range(0, 12345), Eval.now(OptionT.pure[Id](0L))) { (a, accEvalG) =>
    G.pure(a).map2Eval(accEvalG) { _ + _ }
  }
  .value
```

Note: even though `Applicative` is summoned, since `OptionT` is a `Monad`, it implements `FlatMap` too. And since it doesn't override `map2Eval`, the one from `FlatMap` is used.

Show null compatibility with scala string interpolator

We have a lot of java things we interact and they can throw exceptions or have null in certain pojo fields to where if we use them in a string interpolator it will just print null

```scala
val foo = Foo(a, null)
log.info(s"found a foo ${foo.secondField)}")

val exception = new RuntimeException()
log.error(s"something happened ${exception.getMessage}", exception)
```

We tried to migrate to show but it blows up and does not give you a good error message to indicate where the problem is.
I asked in Discord and here's the feedback:
1.  don't use nulls (it's very hard to do this especially when integrating with a lot of java teams/libraries)
1.  it's a bug, file a bug (doing that here)
1.  write my own version of show SafeShow and use that.

I've done number 3 and created SafeShow but incorporating it into our legacy monolith is an effort itself so I thought I'd open this to see if anything could be done in cats itself.  (I've basically duplicated the show syntax, interpolator and use the [typename](https://github.com/tpolecat/typename) as required field for constructing the Show type class instance using SafeShow and log the typename and the exception when we recover from NPE before printing "null").  This is not something I really want to maintain although it's not that much code (less than 100 lines of main code).  Also most of our libraries just use plain Show so we have to go and change all those as well.

The ability to adopt and use things from cats for newbies is also a hurdle.  When do I use our safe show and when do we not.
It's a lot for newbies to absorb and makes the interop with Java stuff harder.  It's also a hurdle when things that seem like they should be equivalent are not.

WorkerThread#getSuspendedFiberCount throws NPE

A few people reported a case when `WorkerThread#getSuspendedFiberCount` throws an NPE. Here, for example, is an issue in the otel4s repo: https://github.com/typelevel/otel4s/issues/973.

The stacktrace:
```
java.lang.NullPointerException: Cannot invoke "cats.effect.unsafe.WeakBag.size()" because "this.fiberBag" is null
 at cats.effect.unsafe.WorkerThread.getSuspendedFiberCount(WorkerThread.scala:1019)
 at cats.effect.unsafe.WorkStealingThreadPool.getSuspendedFiberCount(WorkStealingThreadPool.scala:842)
 at cats.effect.unsafe.metrics.WorkStealingThreadPoolMetrics$$anon$1.suspendedFiberCount(WorkStealingThreadPoolMetrics.scala:265)
```

The `fiberBag` can be unreferenced when the previously blocked thread is removed from the core pool: https://github.com/typelevel/cats-effect/blob/v3.6.1/core/jvm/src/main/scala/cats/effect/unsafe/WorkerThread.scala#L724.

And the [getSuspendedFiberCount](https://github.com/typelevel/cats-effect/blob/v3.6.1/core/jvm/src/main/scala/cats/effect/unsafe/WorkerThread.scala#L1018-L1019) is defined as:
```scala
def getSuspendedFiberCount(): Int =
  fiberBag.size
```

The Polling System CPU overhead

The new polling system looks interesting for the cases when there are libraries which use it, but when I updated my app to CE 3.6, I faced a significant CPU increase (more than 70%). According to the profiler it is caused by EPoll-related calls from the Cats Effect code. When I overrode the polling system to `SleepSystem` in the main class the problem has gone and the CPU consumption was back top normal:

```scala
import cats.effect.unsafe.{PollingSystem, SleepSystem}
....
object Main extends IOApp {
    override protected def pollingSystem: PollingSystem = SleepSystem
    .......
}
```

Thare are still a lot of apps and libraries which use the polling mechanisms directly and can't be re-implemented to use the new feature. It means that users can see some performance degradations just because they bumped Cats Effect to 3.6. Do you have  any ideas how this issue can be addressed? Does every app which doesn't rely on the polling system require such explicit change?

Allow `IO.onError` to be passed a total function

#4121 deprecated `IO.onError(f: Throwable => IO[Unit])` and added `def onError(pf: PartialFunction[Throwable, IO[Unit]])` to be used instead.

[Normally total functions can be passed in place of a partial function and Scala will automatically create a partial function wrapper for it](https://scastie.scala-lang.org/4yClxhLmQ5mq8ZoO6f1Oag). In other words, we should be able to call the partial function variant of `onError` with a total function. [Here's a scastie snippet showing that expected behaviour of `onError` with `Resource`](https://scastie.scala-lang.org/JGfAkqBCTRu82iLCMDMSeA).

Unfortunately, [when calling `IO.onError` with a total function in CE 3.6.0, there is deprecation warning](https://scastie.scala-lang.org/FxT9UQcvSSyDXP06okStYQ). This is inconsistent with earlier versions or the behaviour of `ApplicativeError.onError`.

It'd be great to make `IO.onError` not generate a deprecation warning when passed a total function.

[Proposed fix from discord](https://discord.com/channels/632277896739946517/632278585700384799/1355235154351620257) is to mark the deprecated version of `onError` as package private.

External queue can starve timers/pollers

If the local queue becomes empty, the worker transitions to checking the external queue.
https://github.com/typelevel/cats-effect/blob/a49a0286b1530047f7aaf1e0964339d88c73dce8/core/jvm/src/main/scala/cats/effect/unsafe/WorkerThread.scala#L824-L827

If it finds work there, it will immediately transition back to crunching the local queue.
https://github.com/typelevel/cats-effect/blob/a49a0286b1530047f7aaf1e0964339d88c73dce8/core/jvm/src/main/scala/cats/effect/unsafe/WorkerThread.scala#L668-L669

This offers no opportunity to fire timers or poll for I/O.

Dropping queue high memory usage I've been benchmarking otel4s [BatchSpanProcessor.scala](https://github.com/typelevel/otel4s/blob/main/sdk/trace/src/main/scala/org/typelevel/otel4s/sdk/trace/processor/BatchSpanProcessor.scala) and noticed a heavy memory consumption. One of the suspects is the dropping queue. Switching to `Queue.bounded` dropped memory usage by 30-40%. I added dropping queue to the [benchmarks](https://github.com/typelevel/cats-effect/blob/series/3.x/benchmarks/src/main/scala/cats/effect/benchmarks/QueueBenchmark.scala) and run a few: ``` Benchmark (size) Mode Cnt Score Error Units boundedAsyncEnqueueDequeueMany 32768 thrpt 10 304.622 ± 3.166 ops/s boundedAsyncEnqueueDequeueMany:gc.alloc.rate 32768 thrpt 10 3922.005 ± 40.728 MB/sec boundedAsyncEnqueueDequeueMany:gc.alloc.rate.norm 32768 thrpt 10 13501842.848 ± 2.361 B/op boundedAsyncEnqueueDequeueMany:gc.count 32768 thrpt 10 1077.000 counts boundedAsyncEnqueueDequeueMany:gc.time 32768 thrpt 10 632.000 ms boundedAsyncEnqueueDequeueOne 32768 thrpt 10 307.547 ± 3.854 ops/s boundedAsyncEnqueueDequeueOne:gc.alloc.rate 32768 thrpt 10 3729.077 ± 46.477 MB/sec boundedAsyncEnqueueDequeueOne:gc.alloc.rate.norm 32768 thrpt 10 12715370.817 ± 2.316 B/op boundedAsyncEnqueueDequeueOne:gc.count 32768 thrpt 10 1256.000 counts boundedAsyncEnqueueDequeueOne:gc.time 32768 thrpt 10 709.000 ms droppingEnqueueDequeueMany 32768 thrpt 10 149.328 ± 6.145 ops/s droppingEnqueueDequeueMany:gc.alloc.rate 32768 thrpt 10 6047.413 ± 248.586 MB/sec droppingEnqueueDequeueMany:gc.alloc.rate.norm 32768 thrpt 10 42468733.794 ± 4.730 B/op droppingEnqueueDequeueMany:gc.count 32768 thrpt 10 897.000 counts droppingEnqueueDequeueMany:gc.time 32768 thrpt 10 1296.000 ms droppingEnqueueDequeueOne 32768 thrpt 10 175.001 ± 6.565 ops/s droppingEnqueueDequeueOne:gc.alloc.rate 32768 thrpt 10 6824.599 ± 256.766 MB/sec droppingEnqueueDequeueOne:gc.alloc.rate.norm 32768 thrpt 10 40895829.028 ± 4.461 B/op droppingEnqueueDequeueOne:gc.count 32768 thrpt 10 1567.000 counts droppingEnqueueDequeueOne:gc.time 32768 thrpt 10 884.000 ms ``` The dropping queue's normalized (per operation) allocation is 3x more than the bounded queue. ### JFR data **Bounded queue:** **Dropping queue:** ### Affected queues The dropping queue is backed by the `AbstractQueue`. The circular buffer and bounded queue (concurrent-based variant) also use `AbstractQueue`, so they should show heavy-memory usage symptoms, too.

Default constructor for `MapRef`

IO.onError is inconsistent with ApplicativeError

The `onError` that you get from `ApplicativeError` has this signature:

```
def onError[A](fa: F[A])(pf: PartialFunction[E, F[Unit]]): F[A]
```

While the "native" one in `IO` doesn't take a `PartialFunction`:

```
def onError(f: Throwable => IO[Unit]): IO[A]
```

That could lead to some surprises if you would rewrite a piece of code from tagless final to `IO`, or if you are just used to using the `onError` extension method in other code.

RangeResult.ofSource: mismatch in Content-Range and body for multiple ranges

When a Range header contains multiple byte-ranges whose gap exceeds Range.minimumDistance (80 bytes), `RangeResult.ofSource` sets `Content-Range` to all requested ranges, but the response body only contains data for the first range — the rest are silently dropped, producing an inconsistent response.

```scala
val content = ("0123456789" * 20) // 200 bytes, index 0..199
RangeResult.ofSource(
      entityLength = content.length.toLong,
```

### Expected Behavior
If app is behind multiple reverse proxies, `X-Forwarded-For`will correctly be multiple values but `X-Forwarded-Proto` might not be (which is actually what is [recommended in Django framework](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.8/ref/settings/#secure-proxy-ssl-header) as it states reverse proxy to strip out all previous `X-Forwarded-Proto` and insert single flag.). In this case, where two are of different length and `X-Forwarded-Proto` is single value, it should use that value for `secure` flag instead of [defaulting to `false`](https://github.com/playframework/playframework/blob/1fd78c95ebb070bf7dc731335c755a97ef3ae5c0/framework/src/play-server/src/main/scala/play/core/server/common/ForwardedHeaderHandler.scala#L188).

### Actual Behavior
`secure` flag is defaulted to `false`.

------

My current setting is using heroku on top of cloudflare. Heroku seems to strip out / don't add to existing `X-Forwarded-Proto`, which is set by cloudflare in front. So even when we use Full SSL for cloudflare (which mandates ssl connection for both heroku - cloudflare and cloudflare - client), `X-Forwarded-Proto` is still set to single `https` value, which fails play parser for `secure` flag and make it defaulted to to `false`.

support escaping space in string literals using `\s` Java multiline blocks supports `\s` escape sequence. https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/21/text-blocks/index.html > The \s escape sequence simple translates to space (\040, ASCII character 32, white space.) Since escape sequences don't get translated until after incident space stripping, \s can act as fence to prevent the stripping of trailing white space. Using \s at the end of each line in the following example, guarantees that each line is exactly six characters long. > >```java >String colors = """ > red \s > green\s > blue \s > """; >``` Scala doesn't support `\s` escape sequence. Scala 2 compiler crashes Scala 3 compiler says > invalid escape '\s' not one of [\b, \t, \n, \f, \r, \\, \", \', \uxxxx] at index 4 in "aaa \s bbb". Use \\ for literal \. Given that `\s` is not reserved, the compiler could support by simply replacing with `\s` At first glance, this should be trivial to fix. This wouldn't work in non-interpolated multiline string literals and in raw string literals because they don't support escape sequences. Still, it could still be a nice addition.

Proposal: Deprecate `jpath` and `getJPath` extensions to `SourceFile` for removal

`Quotes` currently defines `jpath` and `getJPath` extensions to `SourceFile`:

https://github.com/scala/scala3/blob/83306a83cde62182b18c2bc20a8afd79ecced712/library/src/scala/quoted/Quotes.scala#L5541-L5549

(`jpath` is already deprecated due to nullability, but with no message around if/when it'll be removed)

These expose a `java.nio.file.Path`, which:
- doesn't exist for our "virtual" files (`VirtualFile`)
- isn't supported on Scala.js (per @sjrd)

They encourage users to write code that only works in specific contexts, i.e., they're a pit of failure. See https://github.com/scalameta/metals/issues/7460 for an example: a piece of code that works in the compiler but fails in the presentation compiler.

I suggest deprecating them in 3.9.0 for removal, with the recommended alternative being the existing `path` extension that returns an opaque string.

If we get requests to distinguish files that exist on disk from those that don't, we could later expose `isVirtual` or something like that, but I suggest only doing this if there's a real need.

Queue.offerAll silently drops items when paired takers are already interrupted

Was looking at why we occasionally see fewer items in a `Queue` than were offered, when the offering fiber races with takers being interrupted. Found that `offerAll` doesn't check the result of `unsafeCompletePromise`, while `tryOffer` does — so a value paired with an already-interrupted taker is silently lost.

The relevant block in `Queue.scala`:

https://github.com/zio/zio/blob/96b8532f9cdf341e2583794969a2d20ea796594d/core/shared/src/main/scala/zio/Queue.scala#L194-L202

```scala
override def offerAll[A1 <: A](as: Iterable[A1])(implicit trace: Trace): UIO[Chunk[A1]] =
  ZIO.suspendSucceed {
    if (shutdownFlag.get) ZIO.interrupt
    else {
      val pTakers                = if (queue.isEmpty()) unsafePollN(takers, as.size) else Chunk.empty
      val (forTakers, remaining) = as.splitAt(pTakers.size)
      (pTakers zip forTakers).foreach { case (taker, item) =>
        unsafeCompletePromise(taker, item)   // <-- return value ignored
      }
```

If a taker promise is already completed (because the taker fiber was interrupted), `unsafeCompletePromise` returns `false`. `offerAll` ignores that and moves on; the item neither reaches a live taker nor goes back into the queue.

Compare with `tryOffer` a few lines above, which correctly retries against the next taker on `false`:

https://github.com/zio/zio/blob/96b8532f9cdf341e2583794969a2d20ea796594d/core/shared/src/main/scala/zio/Queue.scala#L177-L184

```scala
@tailrec def offeredToTaker(): Boolean = {
  val taker = takers.poll()
  if (taker eq null) false
  else if (unsafeCompletePromise(taker, a)) true
  else offeredToTaker()
}
```

Reproducer (scala-cli):

```scala
//> using scala 3.8.3
//> using dep dev.zio::zio:2.1.26

import zio.*

object Repro extends ZIOAppDefault:
  def run =
    for
      queue   <- Queue.bounded[Int](10)
      fibers  <- ZIO.foreach(1 to 5)(_ => queue.take.fork)
      _       <- ZIO.sleep(50.millis)              // give takers a chance to register
      _       <- ZIO.foreach(fibers)(_.interrupt)  // and then interrupt all of them
      remaining <- queue.offerAll(1 to 5)
      items     <- queue.takeAll
      _ <- ZIO.debug(s"remaining=$remaining items=$items total=${items.size + remaining.size}")
    yield ()
```

The race is timing-dependent (the fibers must be interrupted while still parked in `takers` and not yet polled), but conceptually the loss is unavoidable in the current shape of the code.

A minimal fix would be to collect the items where `unsafeCompletePromise` returned `false` and prepend them to `remaining` before falling through to the queue offer path. Happy to PR.

zio TMap.takeFirst silently drops remaining bucket elements after match

## What



## Project
zio (core, stm)

## Category
STM, data-loss

## Severity
wrong-code (silent data loss)

## Versions
All current versions of ZIO 2.x with STM

## Reproducer

In `TMap.scala`, the `takeFirst` method at lines 394-431:

```scala
def takeFirst[A](pf: PartialFunction[(K, V), A]): USTM[A] =
  ZSTM
    .Effect[Any, Nothing, Option[A]] { (journal, _, _) =>
      // ...
      while (i < capacity && (result == None)) {
        val bucket   = buckets.array(i).unsafeGet(journal)
        val recreate = bucket.exists(isDefinedAt)

        if (recreate) {
          var newBucket = List.empty[(K, V)]
          val it        = bucket.iterator

          while (it.hasNext && (result == None)) {  // exits early when match found
            val pair = it.next()
            if (isDefinedAt(pair) && result == None) {
              result = Some(pf(pair))
            } else {
              newBucket = pair :: newBucket
            }
          }
          buckets.array(i).unsafeSet(journal, newBucket)  // overwrites with INCOMPLETE bucket
        }

        i += 1
      }
      // ...
    }
```

When a matching element is found in the middle of a bucket, the inner while loop exits immediately. All remaining elements in that bucket's iterator are never added to `newBucket`. Then on line 421, the bucket is overwritten with `newBucket` which is missing those trailing elements.

Example: bucket contains `[(a,1), (b,2), (c,3)]` and `pf` matches `(b,2)`:
- `(a,1)` is added to newBucket (does not match)
- `(b,2)` matches, result is set, inner loop exits
- `(c,3)` is NEVER iterated, never added to newBucket
- Bucket is overwritten with just `[(a,1)]` -- `(c,3)` is lost forever

## Expected behavior
After taking the first matching element, all non-matching elements in the same bucket should be preserved.

## Actual behavior
Elements that appear after the first match within the same hash bucket are silently dropped from the map. This is data corruption that occurs only when multiple keys hash to the same bucket.

## Fix
After the inner loop exits, the remaining iterator elements must be drained into `newBucket`:
```scala
// after inner while loop:
while (it.hasNext) {
  newBucket = it.next() :: newBucket
}
buckets.array(i).unsafeSet(journal, newBucket)
```

## Duplicate check
- Searched `findings/bugs/` for "TMap", "takeFirst", "bucket", "drop" -- no matches
- Not related to bug-106 (Schedule.dayOfMonth), bug-107 (Queue.offerAll), bug-108 (Queue.sliding), or bug-112 (Stream.sliding)

## File
`/Users/mikhailmurunov/scala-oss/projects/zio/core/shared/src/main/scala/zio/stm/TMap.scala` lines 394-431

## Minimal runnable reproducer

Save as `repro.scala` and run `scala-cli run --server=false repro.scala`:

```scala

//> using dep dev.zio::zio:2.1.21

import zio._
import zio.stm._

final case class K(name: String) {
  override def hashCode(): Int = 0  // force all keys into same bucket
}

object App extends ZIOAppDefault {
  def run = for {
    map <- TMap.make(K("a") -> 1, K("b") -> 2, K("c") -> 3).commit
    sizeBefore <- map.size.commit
    taken <- map.takeFirst { case (k, v) if v == 2 => v }.commit
    sizeAfter <- map.size.commit
    remaining <- map.toList.commit
    _ <- Console.printLine(s"taken=$taken")
    _ <- Console.printLine(s"sizeBefore=$sizeBefore  sizeAfter=$sizeAfter")
    _ <- Console.printLine(s"remaining=${remaining.map { case (k, v) => k.name -> v }.sortBy(_._1)}")
    _ <- Console.printLine(s"expected remaining = List((a,1), (c,3))  -> size 2")
    _ <- Console.printLine(
      if (remaining.size == 2) "RESULT: NOT_REPRODUCED (all non-matching kept)"
      else s"RESULT: REPRODUCED — bucket lost ${2 - remaining.size} element(s) after match"
```

Invalid cast in `Macros.traceExpr`

`Macros.traceExpr`:

https://github.com/zio/zio/blob/bf00fac8aae5bf09c0e94cc4705f14b53f3dfa32/stacktracer/src/main/scala-3/zio/internal/stacktracer/Macros.scala#L72-L73

interprets an expression of type `String` as `Tracer.instance.Type`. This is malformed:  `String` is not a subtype of `Tracer.instance.Type` currently, because `Tracer.instance` is currently just `Tracer` with no information about its type member. The macro works "out of luck", but that's invalid code.

Note: the problem can't be fixed simply by adding the `{type Type = String}` to `Tracer.instance`. This fixes the macro itself but breaks implicit search.

I found that problem while working on https://github.com/scala/scala3/issues/25690 and https://github.com/scala/scala3/pull/25731.

`ZIO.memoize` interrupts awaiting fibers

Hi. We have discovered the behavior of  `ZIO.memoize`  that may lead to an error: interrupting of running memoize can lead to interruptions of awaiting fibers. The code that reproduces the behavior:

```scala
//> using scala 2.13.18
//> using dep "dev.zio::zio:2.1.24"

import zio.{Promise, ZIO, ZIOAppDefault}

object Main extends ZIOAppDefault {

  override def run: ZIO[Any, Any, Any] =
    for {
      memoWasStarted <- Promise.make[Nothing, Unit]

      memo <- ZIO.memoize((_: Any) => memoWasStarted.complete(ZIO.unit) *> ZIO.never)

      fstCall <- memo("call (should be memoized)").fork
      _       <- memoWasStarted.await
      sndCall <- memo("call (should be memoized)").fork
      _       <- fstCall.interrupt
      _       <- sndCall.join // interrupt sndCall!
    } yield ()

}
```

Error: 
```java
timestamp=<timestamp> level=ERROR thread=#zio-fiber-183479508 message="" cause="java.lang.InterruptedException: Interrupted by thread "zio-fiber-1416249622"
```

Current behavior is:
1. Fiber1 start evaluation `V` in memoize by `K1` -> Start evaluation `V`
2. Fiber2 start evaluation `V` in memoize by `K1` -> Fiber2 waits already started `V`
3. Fiber1 interrupt -> interrupt `V` -> interrupt Fiber2

But I didn't expect that interrupting Fiber1 would lead to interrupting Fiber2. Sugested behavior:
1. Fiber1 start evaluation `V` in memoize by `K1` -> Start evaluation `V`
2. Fiber2 start evaluation `V` in memoize by `K1` -> Fiber2 waits already started `V`
3. Fiber1 interrupt -> evaluation `V` continues (because the result of `V` is still waiting in not interrupted Fiber2)

Add ZStream.fromInputStreamInterruptible

The implementation of `ZStream.fromInputStream` will not interrupt reading from the underlying stream if the fiber gets interrupted.
As long as the underlying call to `InputStream.read` blocks, the fiber interruption blocks until `InputStream.read` returns.

A version that uses `ZIO.attemptBlockingCancelable` instead of `ZIO.attemptBlocking` should be provided for use cases where reading from the `InputStream` is expected to block for longer and need to be interrupted (i.e. timeout or similar)

IMO this looks like a bug and the interruptible version should be the default but that change might break existing code. At least this behaviour should  be documented for `ZIO.fromInputStream`

 See also: zio/zio-process#425

Optimize shifting executors (ZIO.blocking)

`ZIO.blocking`  requires four loops of the FiberRuntime just to shift work:

1. Get blocking executor: [FiberRef.currentBlockingExecutor.getWith](https://github.com/zio/zio/blob/series/2.x/core/shared/src/main/scala/zio/ZIO.scala#L2889-L2890)
2. Call `onExecutor` -> `descriptorWith`: https://github.com/zio/zio/blob/series/2.x/core/shared/src/main/scala/zio/ZIO.scala#L4452-L4454
3. Shift to the new executor `ZIO.shift`: https://github.com/zio/zio/blob/series/2.x/core/shared/src/main/scala/zio/ZIO.scala#L4803-L4820
4. Either call `unshift` or `shift` again: https://github.com/zio/zio/blob/series/2.x/core/shared/src/main/scala/zio/ZIO.scala#L4452-L4454

ZStream mapZIOPar freezes when output is only partially consumed

ZStream `.mapZIOPar` (and also `.mapZIOParUnordered`) freezes when followed by `.take` that only consumes a part of the output.

Example:

```scala
val inputStream = ZStream.fromChunks(Seq.fill(1)(Chunk.fill(100)("abc"))*) // Freezes every(?) time
// val inputStream = ZStream.fromChunks(Seq.fill(100)(Chunk.fill(1)("abc"))*) // Mostly passes, freezes only sometimes

inputStream.mapZIOPar(8)(_ => ZIO.unit).take(1).runDrain
```

As noted in the comments, behavior seems to nondeterministically depend on how the input stream is chunked.

Tested with ZIO 2.1.21.
